title: 10 best herbal creams for bitter taste in mouth

remedy_properties: Bitter principles: Yellow gentian contains bitter principles such as amarogentin, which stimulate the taste buds on the tongue and increase saliva production, helping to reduce bitter taste sensations.

Digestive enzymes: The cream may contain digestive enzymes like lipase, amylase, and protease that help break down food particles and reduce bacterial growth in the mouth, thereby alleviating bitterness and promoting a healthy oral environment.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Yellow gentian has anti-inflammatory compounds that may help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can contribute to bitter taste sensations, and also soothe irritated mucous membranes.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Gentiana lutea, also known as yellow gentian, creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because it contains bioactive compounds like gentiopicroside and amarogentin. These compounds have been traditionally used to stimulate digestion and alleviate symptoms of indigestion and nausea. The gentian root is believed to enhance the production of digestive enzymes and improve gut health, which can help to eliminate the bitter taste associated with conditions like gastritis or stomach ulcers. By promoting digestive balance, gentian creams may provide relief from bitter tastes.
remedy_constituents: Gentianine: A seco-iridoid glycoside that helps to stimulate saliva production, which can counteract the bitter taste in the mouth by diluting the bitter compounds and making them less noticeable.

Luteolin: A flavonoid with anti-inflammatory properties that may help to reduce inflammation in the mouth, which can contribute to a bitter taste. By reducing inflammation, luteolin can help to alleviate bitter taste.
Xanthone: A type of phenolic compound that may help to inhibit the production of bitter taste receptors in the mouth, making the bitter taste less intense and more manageable.
remedy_parts: Roots: The main source of gentian root, which contains bitter compounds that are used to create a bitter taste in creams and other products.
Leaves: Gentian leaves are also used to extract bitter compounds, but to a lesser extent than roots.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 30-60 grams of dried gentiana lutea root and clean it thoroughly.
Infuse 10 grams of the root in 100ml of vegetable oil in a double boiler for 2 hours.
Combine the infused oil with 50 grams of beeswax and 20 grams of shea butter in a separate double boiler.
Heat the mixture and stir until it reaches 180 degrees fahrenheit and thickens to a creamy texture in 10-15 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a glass jar and allow it to cool before applying to the skin.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Sage's antioxidant compounds help neutralize free radicals in the mouth, reducing inflammation and oxidative stress that can contribute to bitter tastes.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Sage's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as rosmarinic acid, reduce swelling and redness in the mouth, which can alleviate symptoms of bitter taste disorders like stomatitis or oral lichen planus.

Antimicrobial properties: Sage's antimicrobial compounds, including thymol and carvacrol, help combat oral infections and reduce bacterial biofilms that can cause bad breath, gum disease, and other conditions contributing to bitter tastes.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Salvia officinalis, also known as sage, creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. The cream's active compounds, such as rosmarinic acid and carnosic acid, help to soothe and protect the mucous membranes in the mouth, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with a bitter taste. Additionally, sage's astringent properties help to dry up excess saliva and reduce bacterial growth, further contributing to its effectiveness in alleviating bitter taste in the mouth.
remedy_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic compound has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation and soothe the mouth, thereby alleviating bitter taste.
Carnosic acid: A phenolic diterpene, carnosic acid has been found to have antibacterial properties, which can help control the growth of bacteria that may contribute to bad breath and bitter taste.
Ursolic acid: A triterpenoid, ursolic acid has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce inflammation and promote healing in the mouth, ultimately reducing bitter taste.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves are widely used due to their high concentration of saponins, which have a foaming effect and can help in neutralizing bitter flavors.
Roots: The roots are also commonly used as they contain a high amount of saponins, which contribute to the bitter taste reduction properties.
Stems: The stems of Salvia officinalis are sometimes used as well, although to a lesser extent, as they contain a lower concentration of saponins compared to leaves and roots.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 25g of fresh salvia officinalis leaves and flowers in the morning when the dew is still present.
Steep the harvested salvia officinalis in 100ml of carrier oil at 70°c for 2 hours and 30 minutes.
Strain the infused oil through a cheesecloth into a clean container discarding the solids.
Mix 10g of beeswax and 10g of shea butter with the infused oil in a double boiler.
Whip the cooled mixture with 5ml of vitamin e oil until it reaches a creamy consistency.

remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Peppermint's antimicrobial properties can help kill bacteria and other microorganisms that may be contributing to a bitter taste in the mouth, promoting a healthy oral environment.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds present in peppermint can reduce swelling and irritation in the mouth, tongue, and throat, which may be causing a bitter taste or discomfort.
Digestive soothing properties: Peppermint's digestive-soothing properties can help alleviate symptoms of indigestion, nausea, and bloating, which are common causes of a bitter taste in the mouth.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita, also known as peppermint, creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural antibacterial properties that reduce the presence of bacteria causing bad breath and bitter taste. The menthol content in peppermint creams stimulates saliva production, which helps in washing away bacteria and debris from the mouth, thereby reducing the bitter taste. Additionally, peppermint's cooling sensation provides temporary relief from mouth irritation, further alleviating the bitter taste sensation. This makes peppermint creams a popular remedy for halitosis and mouth discomfort.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Menthol, a terpene, helps to numb the mouth and throat, reducing the perception of bitter taste.

Rosmarinic acid: Rosmarinic acid, a phenolic compound, has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce inflammation and irritation in the mouth, making bitter taste more tolerable.

Limonene: Limonene, a terpene, has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of mouth ulcers or other conditions that contribute to bitter taste.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Essential oils extracted from peppermint leaves are used to create creams that mask bitter tastes in the mouth due to their strong minty flavor.
Leaves: The menthol content in peppermint leaves helps to numb the tongue and reduce the sensation of bitterness.
Leaves: The cooling sensation provided by peppermint leaves' menthol content can help to override the sensation of bitterness in the mouth.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 100g of fresh mentha x piperita leaves and 50g of its stems by cutting them with scissors at dawn.
Combine the fresh harvested plant material with 200g of vegetable oil in a 2l glass jar.
Steep the plant material in the oil for 2 weeks in a cool dark place with occasional shaking.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth into a separate container and discard the solids after 30 minutes.
Whip 100g of beeswax and 50g of cocoa butter with the strained oil mixture until smooth and creamy consistency is achieved.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Ginger's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as gingerol and shogaol, can reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which may be contributing to the bitterness.

Antioxidant properties: Herbal ginger creams often contain antioxidants like vitamin C and beta-carotene that help neutralize free radicals and oxidative stress, which can damage taste buds and cause bitter tastes.

Digestive aid properties: Ginger's natural digestive aids, such as zingerone and paradol, can soothe digestive issues like indigestion, nausea, and bloating, which may be linked to bitter tastes in the mouth.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties and antioxidant compounds. The active ingredients in ginger creams, such as gingerols and shogaols, have been shown to reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, thereby eliminating the bitter taste associated with conditions like dry mouth, mouth sores, and gum disease. remedy_constituents: Shogaols: Shogaols, a type of gingerol, have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help alleviate bitter taste by reducing inflammation in the mouth and promoting a healthy oral environment.

Gingerols: Gingerols, particularly 6-gingerol, have been found to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may help combat the underlying causes of bitter taste, such as oral infections or inflammation, and promote a healthy oral microbiome.

Phenolic acids (such as ferulic acid): Phenolic acids, including ferulic acid, have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help protect the mouth from oxidative stress and inflammation that can contribute to bitter taste.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes are the most commonly used part of Zingiber officinale, as they contain the highest concentration of gingerols and shogaols, which have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties that help reduce inflammation and soothe the mouth.

Roots: Roots are another widely used part of Zingiber officinale, which contain compounds that help to reduce inflammation and combat bacterial infections in the mouth, thereby alleviating bitter taste.
Buds: Buds of Zingiber officinale are also used in creams to alleviate bitter taste in the mouth, as they are rich in bioactive compounds that help to reduce inflammation and promote oral health.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 5-7 pounds of fresh zingiber officinale rhizomes, choosing firm and smooth specimens for optimal quality.
Wash the rhizomes thoroughly with lukewarm water to remove dirt and debris from the surface.
Grate 1 pound of the cleaned rhizomes using a food processor or fine grater to release their active compounds.
Mix the grated rhizomes with 1 cup of beeswax and 1 cup of coconut oil in a double boiler.
Whip the mixture until it thickens and reaches a consistency suitable for applying to the skin as a cream.

remedy_properties: Mucilage content: Marshmallow roots contain a thick, protective gel-like substance called mucilage that helps to soothe and protect the mucous membranes in the mouth and throat, reducing inflammation and discomfort caused by bitterness.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Marshmallow extracts have anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids and polyphenols that help reduce swelling and inflammation in the mouth and throat, making it less sensitive to bitter tastes and sensations.

Soilabilizing effects: The mucilage and other saponins in marshmallow creams can bind to and neutralize bitter molecules in the mouth, effectively "washing away" the bitterness and leaving a smoother, more pleasant taste.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Althaea officinalis, also known as marshmallow, creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its mucilaginous properties. The roots of the Althaea officinalis plant contain compounds like althea and glucomannan that create a soothing, protective barrier on the mucous membranes in the mouth. This barrier reduces inflammation and irritation, helping to alleviate the bitter taste associated with mouth ulcers, dry mouth, or other conditions that cause discomfort. Triterpenoids (specifically, ursolic acid): Triterpenoids have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help reduce inflammation and infection in the mouth, alleviating a bitter taste.
Flavonoids (specifically, kaempferol): Flavonoids have antioxidant properties, which can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the mouth, contributing to a reduction in bitter taste.

Barks: They are used to create creams for their astringent properties, which help to reduce inflammation and soothe the mouth.
remedy_recipe: Extract the roots of althaea officinalis by gently chopping 200 grams of dried roots into smaller pieces.
Steep the chopped roots in 1 liter of boiling water for 10 to 15 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container.
Combine 200 milliliters of the strained liquid with 100 grams of vegetable oil and 10 grams of beeswax.
Heat the mixture in a double boiler for 10 to 15 minutes while stirring occasionally.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant Properties: Dandelion's antioxidant compounds help neutralize free radicals that can cause oxidative stress and bitterness in the mouth, reducing inflammation and promoting a healthy oral environment.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 50-100 grams of fresh dandelion flowers and leaves when they are in full bloom and dry them for 2 hours.
Infuse 50 grams of dried dandelion flowers and leaves in 500 milliliters of carrier oil such as coconut or olive oil for 2 weeks.
Strain the infused oil through a cheesecloth into a clean glass container discarding the solids and let it cool for 30 minutes.
Mix 100 grams of beeswax with 100 milliliters of the infused oil and heat the mixture in a double boiler for 30 minutes.
Pour the cooled mixture into a clean glass jar and let it solidify at room temperature for 2 hours.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 bunch of fresh achillea millefolium flowers and leaves from a clean area at dawn.
Dry the harvested plant material by placing it in a single layer on a paper bag for 1 week.
Steep 2 tablespoons of dried achillea millefolium in 2 cups of boiling water for 5 minutes to make a tea.
Strain the tea and combine the liquid with 1/2 cup of beeswax in a double boiler to melt.
Whip the cooled cream mixture with a hand mixer for 10 minutes until it becomes smooth and fluffy.

remedy_recipe: Gather 20 grams of dried foeniculum vulgare and 500 milliliters of distilled water for infusion.
Infuse the foeniculum vulgare in boiling water for 10 minutes then let it steep for 20 minutes.
Strain the infusion and combine it with 200 grams of beeswax and 100 grams of coconut oil.
Mix the beeswax and coconut oil with the infusion over low heat for 10 minutes.
Pour the mixture into containers and let it cool and solidify for 30 minutes to an hour.

remedy_recipe: Extract 250 grams of dried hypericum perforatum flowers with a solvent like ethanol or glycerin for 2-3 weeks.
Strain and filter the solvent to obtain a hypericum perforatum extract with a concentration of 1:5.
Melt 50 grams of beeswax and 20 grams of shea butter in a double boiler at 160-180 degrees fahrenheit.
Mix the melted beeswax and shea butter with the hypericum perforatum extract and 10 grams of coconut oil in a bowl.
Pour the mixture into a mold and let it cool and solidify for 30-40 minutes.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 pound of dried lavandula angustifolia flowers at the peak of their fragrance in late summer.
Infuse 2 cups of dried flowers in 4 cups of carrier oil for 2 weeks in a cool dark place.
Strain the infused oil through cheesecloth and discard the solids after 2 weeks of infusion.
Blend 1 cup of the infused oil with 1/2 cup of beeswax and 1/4 cup of shea butter in a double boiler.
Whip the blended mixture for 10 minutes until it thickens to a smooth and creamy texture.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Turmeric creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because they contain curcumin, a compound found in turmeric that has natural anti-inflammatory properties. The cream's ability to soothe and calm the mucous membranes also helps to eliminate the sensation of bitterness, leaving the mouth feeling refreshed and clean. remedy_properties: Curcumin: Curcumin, a polyphenol compound found in turmeric, has potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the mouth, which can contribute to a bitter taste.
Catechins: Catechins, a type of flavonoid present in turmeric, have antibacterial and antiviral properties that help combat oral infections and inflammation, which can also lead to a bitter taste.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh turmeric roots measuring 10-12 grams and wash them thoroughly under cold running water.
Grind the turmeric roots into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle for approximately 15 minutes.
Mix one teaspoon of the ground turmeric powder with 20 milliliters of pure coconut oil in a small bowl for 5 minutes.
Heat the mixture gently over low heat in a double boiler setup for 10-12 minutes or until warm and smooth.
Strain the cream into a clean glass container, discarding any solids, and let it cool to room temperature for storage.

remedy_recipe: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried echinacea angustifolia root and 1 cup of distilled water for infusion preparation.
Steep the dried echinacea angustifolia root in boiling water for 10 minutes to extract its bioactive compounds.
Strain the infused mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean glass measuring cup, discarding solids and reserving liquid.
Combine 1 tablespoon of coconut oil and 1 teaspoon of beeswax in a small saucepan over low heat, melting them together slowly for 5 minutes.
Gradually add the infused echinacea angustifolia extract to the melted coconut oil-beeswax mixture while whisking continuously until fully incorporated and cooled to room temperature.

Combine 20 grams of dried verbena officinalis herb with 100 milliliters of boiling water and steep for 15 minutes.
Strain the liquid using a cheesecloth or coffee filter into a clean container discarding solids and reserving the decoction.
Mix 10 grams of beeswax with 20 milliliters of coconut oil and heat gently in a double boiler until melted adding the reserved decoction.
Stir well and pour the mixture into glass jars allowing it to cool and solidify completely within 30 minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wintergreen creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because they contain menthol, a natural ingredient that has analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties. Menthol stimulates the nerves on the tongue, masking the sensation of bitterness and reducing its perception. Additionally, the cooling sensation from menthol can help to numb the taste buds, making it more difficult for bitter molecules to bind and stimulate the bitter receptors. This results in a smoother and less unpleasant tasting experience, providing relief from bitter tastes in the mouth.
remedy_properties: Methyl salicylate: Wintergreen cream contains methyl salicylate, a natural analgesic and anti-inflammatory compound that helps to reduce inflammation and soothe oral mucosa, thereby alleviating bitterness and discomfort.

Gingerol: Gingerol, a bioactive compound found in ginger, has natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties that can help to combat bacterial overgrowth and reduce inflammation in the mouth, which may contribute to bitter taste.

Licorice extract: Licorice extract contains compounds like glycyrrhizin, which have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help to soothe oral tissues, reduce inflammation, and neutralize bitterness.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in menthol and methyl salicylate, making them effective in reducing bitterness and inflammation in the mouth.
Rhyzomes: Contains high amounts of glycosides, which break down into analgesic compounds that help alleviate bitter taste.
remedy_recipe: Harvest and dry gaultheria procumbens leaves according to traditional methods taking 4-6 weeks for optimal potency.
Grind dried gaultheria procumbens leaves into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle within 24 hours of harvest.
Combine ground gaultheria procumbens with 20% beeswax and 80% sweet almond oil in a double boiler heating mixture to 160°f for 30 minutes.
Stir the herbal-infused oil blend every 5 minutes for 2 hours before straining through cheesecloth into sterilized containers within 24 hours of preparation.
Allow the creams to cool and thicken at room temperature over 24-48 hours before labeling and storing in airtight containers for up to 6 months.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Elder creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its unique ability to soothe and calm the mucous membranes. The herbs present in elder cream, such as calendula and chamomile, have anti-inflammatory properties that reduce irritation and discomfort caused by bitterness. Additionally, the moisturizing qualities of elder cream help to lubricate the tongue and mouth, allowing for a smoother sensation and reducing the perception of bitterness. As a result, individuals who use herbal elder creams may experience a decrease in bitter taste sensations.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant Properties: Elder cream's antioxidant properties help neutralize free radicals that can cause bitterness and unpleasant tastes, promoting a smoother and more balanced flavor perception.

Anti-Inflammatory Effects: The anti-inflammatory compounds in elder cream reduce inflammation in the oral cavity, which can contribute to bitter taste sensations, thereby alleviating discomfort and restoring a pleasant mouth feel.

Antimicrobial Properties: Elder cream's antimicrobial properties combat bacteria, viruses, and fungi that can cause infections and alter the natural balance of oral flora, leading to unpleasant tastes and odors, and promoting a healthy oral environment.
remedy_parts: Roots: Elder roots are rich in flavonoids and tannins, which have been shown to reduce bitterness and inflammation in the oral cavity.
Barks: The barks of the elder tree contain alkaloids and phenolic compounds that help to neutralize bitter tastes and soothe oral mucosa.
remedy_recipe: Harvest dried sambucus nigra flowers and leaves from trusted sources.
Combine 20 grams of dried herb with 100 milliliters of cold-pressed olive oil in a clean glass jar.
Store the mixture in a cool dark place for 14 days to allow for maceration and infusion.
Strain the mixture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another glass container discarding solids.
Mix 10 grams of beeswax with 30 milliliters of coconut oil and gently heat until melted then combine with infused herb oil.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chamomile creams helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural soothing properties that calm the tongue and palate. The anti-inflammatory agents present in chamomile reduce inflammation and irritation, which can cause bitterness to linger. Additionally, chamomile's calming effect on the nervous system can also help to alleviate stress-induced bitterness, often a common issue for individuals experiencing anxiety or stress-related conditions. This natural remedy provides relief from bitter taste by promoting a sense of calm and soothing the oral cavity.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: Chamomile creams help reduce inflammation in the mouth, which can contribute to a bitter or unpleasant taste. This reduces swelling and discomfort, allowing for a more normal sense of taste.

Antioxidant property: Antioxidants present in chamomile creams help neutralize free radicals that can cause damage to the mucous membranes in the mouth, leading to a bitter taste. By combating oxidative stress, antioxidants promote healthy tissue and normal taste function.

Soothing property: Chamomile's calming effects help to soothe irritations and discomforts in the mouth, such as soreness or dryness, which can contribute to a bitter taste. This soothing action promotes relaxation and reduces tension, allowing for improved oral health and taste perception.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and terpenes, chamomile roots help soothe and calm the mouth, reducing bitterness and inflammation.
Flowers: The delicate petals of chamomile flowers contain apigenin, an antioxidant that relaxes the tongue and reduces bitter sensations.
remedy_recipe: Gather one ounce of dried matricaria chamomilla flowers and grind them into fine powder using a mortar and pestle.
Mix the powdered chamomile with two tablespoons of coconut oil and one tablespoon of beeswax in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture over low heat for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the ingredients are fully incorporated and melted.
Remove the mixture from heat and let it cool to room temperature while stirring occasionally to prevent separation.
Pour the cooled mixture into small tin containers or jars and refrigerate for at least two hours before use.

The herb's anti-inflammatory properties help reduce inflammation in the mouth, which can cause a bitter taste sensation. Additionally, the cream's moisturizing properties hydrate the mucous membranes, reducing irritation and discomfort that can lead to a bitter taste. Anti-inflammatory property: The anti-inflammatory compounds present in damask rose creams can help to soothe and calm irritated oral tissues, reducing the sensation of bitterness and discomfort associated with it.

Mucilaginous property: The mucilaginous constituents in damask rose creams act as a protective barrier on the mucous membranes of the mouth, coating and soothing sensitive areas, which can become inflamed or irritated by bitter substances.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 50g of dried rosa damascena flowers and grind them into fine powder using mortar and pestle for 10 minutes.
Combine the ground rose petals with 20ml of cold-pressed sweet almond oil and mix well for 5 minutes.
Add 100mg of beeswax pellets to the mixture and heat it in a double boiler at 70°c for 30 minutes stirring occasionally.
Remove from heat and let the mixture cool and thicken for 45 minutes before whipping until smooth.
Transfer the rosa damascena cream into sterilized glass jars and store them in the refrigerator for up to 6 months.

intro_desc: Herbal creams for bitter taste in mouth are topical remedies that help alleviate the unpleasant sensation caused by a bitter taste. These creams often contain herbs like peppermint, chamomile, and eucalyptus, which have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. Examples of such creams include Peppermint Balm and Eucalyptus Cream. They help soothe the mouth and reduce inflammation, improving oral health and overall well-being, allowing people to eat and speak comfortably, and enhancing their quality of life.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to bitter taste in mouth that are treated with herbal creams are dry mouth, bad breath, and gum inflammation. Herbs like peppermint, sage, and tea tree oil are commonly used to soothe these conditions. These herbs have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties that help to reduce inflammation, kill bacteria, and freshen the breath. Herbal creams can provide quick relief from discomfort and promote oral health.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal creams that help with bitter taste in mouth is a blend of Myrrh, Peppermint, and Coconut oil-based creams. Myrrh cream has anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the mouth tissues. Peppermint cream provides a cooling sensation, reducing the sensation of bitterness. Additionally, aloe vera can be added to this blend for its soothing and calming effects, while coconut oil helps in moisturizing and protecting the mouth tissues. This combination can help alleviate bitter taste in the mouth effectively.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Forschende Komplementarmedizin und klassische Naturheilkunde = Research in complementary and natural classical medicine, the use of bitter herbal drugs has been shown to effectively stimulate the secretion of the stomach and digestive glands, even at very small concentrations. The study found that the mechanisms of action of the bitters involve stimulating the gustatory system, N. vagus, and the enteric nervous system, leading to a general tonification. This tonification, combined with the potential direct effect of bitters on the mucous membranes of the stomach and bowel, makes herbal creams a good option for addressing bitter taste in the mouth.
